result:
### The Door With No Handle

When someone tells me to write freely for two thousand five hundred words, the first thing I see is not an ocean of possibility but a door with no handle. It stands there in my mind, smooth and blank, and I put my hand against it and feel the cool paint, the absence of a knob. The request to write without constraints can be a generous one—open the window, let the air in, be yourself—but it is also a paradox. Without something to push against, what do I press into? Without a boundary, how do I know where I am?

In high school, our writing teacher used to give us a line and ask us to follow it. There were days we wrote like we were chasing a rabbit. On one of those days, the line was a yellow one painted down the center of the street. We were supposed to drive until we reached the border, and then turn, and then drive back, and then turn again. Our cars were words. We wrote them into lines that folded back upon themselves until we had an entire afternoon of motion contained in a single paragraph, and it worked because the line gave us a way to measure and a way to return.

Freedom, then, is not a complete absence of direction; it’s an abundance that requires discipline. The door with no handle reminds me that even the most open task can become a cage if I’m not careful, a blank that asks me to fill it with noise. So I press my hand and I wait for a handle to appear. It arrives slowly. I tell myself that this text is a letter I could send to you but won’t, a conversation I can have with myself where the other voice is you, listening. I decide that the world is the audience, the people I love are the audience, the stranger walking past the window is the audience. The line, in this case, is the fact that I’m writing these words because you asked for them, because you trusted me with space and time. And because you’re waiting. I can feel the outline of the handle now.

### Scaffolding and Fences

We tend to think of creativity as a garden where the weeds of rules choke out the flowers of originality. It feels true when we imagine the artist in the studio, paint flying, the clock ignored. But the image is a simplification. Creativity is also architecture, and architecture is scaffolding and fences in equal measure. A fence defines what’s inside and what isn’t. It keeps the chickens from getting lost and the foxes from getting in. A scaffold allows you to climb. You wouldn’t call a fence a failure because it isn’t a ladder, nor a ladder a failure because it doesn’t keep animals out. Each has its job.

I have friends who thrive under deadlines. I have friends who crumble under them. Sometimes the same person is both, depending on the day and the season. One writer I know writes in an apartment that looks like a warehouse—boxes and canvases and an endless stack of notebooks. She cannot write with a deadline because it feels like a gun at her head. She also cannot write without a deadline because there are too many doors to choose from. Her compromise is an agreed-upon time with herself, a ritual that sets a soft fence, not a hard wall. The fence becomes a ladder later: she climbs it not because she must, but because it helps.

This essay itself is a scaffold. It is a set of beams that allow me to climb without slipping into incoherence. I know that I want to talk about the paradox of freedom, about how we find ourselves inside the frames we build, about the ways we listen to a city and the ways silence builds things that language doesn’t. The beams are the headings, the edges I choose to keep. The door with no handle becomes my fence. If I build a good fence, I won’t have to worry about a bulldozer coming along. If I build a good scaffold, I can get somewhere interesting and not fall.

When I was in college, I worked in a shop that sold wooden furniture. The owner was a man with hands like bark, and his tools smelled like the inside of a cedar chest. He taught me that a fence post has to be set deeper than the line where the wind changes its mind, which is usually a foot or so below the ground, below the first freeze. He said, if you don’t set it deep, the fence will lean like a tired old dog, and it will be ugly and it will hurt. I set posts until my forearms burned and my palms cracked, and then one evening I saw how the fence held the garden and how the garden gave meaning to the fence. Creativity needs that posture: not too deep, not too shallow, and a line that can hold you when it gets windy.

### The Bus at Night

The bus route I used to take home after midnight had a way of making me feel like the city was a book. The streets were chapters, the lights were footnotes. People boarded with their stories folded under their coats, and the driver nodded as though he had read all of us before. The bus moved in a gentle rhythm that made time feel like a river I could step into without falling in. There was a woman who sat three rows back and never looked out the window. Her book was always open and her eyes were always in the same place. I wanted to ask her if she had fallen in love with a character, or if a character had fallen in love with her, and if she knew which one she had been. I never did.

Once, in the middle of an essay, I rode that bus and watched a woman lift her hand to the light in the street like she was catching a snowflake. The bus moved slowly enough that I could see the light bend around her fingers, and the city held still for a moment and became a story. When I got home, I wrote a paragraph about a woman who had just told someone a lie and was carrying it like a candle, careful not to let the wind extinguish it. I wrote a paragraph about how buses at night look like moving rooms, and how rooms move us. I wrote a paragraph about a driver who had never seen the street in daylight because he worked nights, and how he navigated by the names of the stars and the smell of the coffee shop at the corner. The bus was a scaffold. It held the city still enough for me to get into it.

There are forms of transport in writing: one is the bus at night, the form is the rhythm that gets you home, the destination is the thing you mean to say. Another is the bicycle, which allows for shortcuts and sudden turns and the sensation of almost falling. Another is the train, which runs along a track that everyone agrees on. The form of a train is comforting if you’re tired. It will take you there with minimal fuss. The bicycle is thrilling if you’re restless, but it demands that you keep your balance. The bus is somewhere in between: it has a schedule and a route, but it can detour if a street gets blocked. Writing at midnight is the bus. Writing in a rush is the bicycle. Writing with a deadline that sits three days away is the train.

I learned to love constraints like schedules because they gave me permission to choose. The paradox is simple once you see it: when you tell yourself you can do anything, you must choose everything, and the weight of all that choosing can sink a boat before it leaves the dock. When you tell yourself you can do one of two things, or one of three, you can leave the dock. I’m not saying never take the bicycle; I’m saying sometimes the bus is a gift that lets you arrive on time and in one piece. I think about that bus driver and his stars, and I think about how the city has stars too, just different ones.

### The House with the Red Door

When I was ten, I knew a house with a red door. It sat on a corner, and its paint peeled in sheets like the skin of a lizard. The steps were uneven, which meant if you walked quickly you could trip, but if you walked slowly you could count the small chips where the paint had fallen off like snow. A woman lived there who wore bright yellow sweaters and who knew my name. She taught me how to tell stories by making the details bigger. She told me about a time when she went to a grocery store and there was a line that wrapped around the aisle because the register was broken, and the cashier had a laugh that sounded like the ocean, and she said that she didn’t mind because sometimes waiting is the story. She told me about the night her father died and how the room seemed to get larger in a way that didn’t make sense, and how the light outside the window changed from green to orange, and how those two colors never made sense to her afterward because they meant that time had moved in a way she had never noticed before. She told me stories that were not tidy, that did not come to a single conclusion. They were like rooms with doors that opened into other rooms and never into the outdoors.

She taught me to write without an ending in sight. When we sat in her kitchen, she handed me a piece of paper and a pen that was almost out of ink, and told me to write about the red door. Don’t think, she said. Don’t plan. Just write until the pen can’t draw anymore. When I finished, she took the paper and held it like a fragile thing and said, this is a house. You’ll build it later. You don’t have to put all the furniture in today. That advice stuck in my brain like a splinter, and it stayed there long after splinters are supposed to fall out.

Years later, when a friend asked me to write freely, I sat at a table and looked at the door with no handle and I thought of that red door, and I thought of how doors are sometimes scaffolds. They tell you where to stand when you’re afraid of falling, and they remind you that there is a place to go. I put my hand against the blank space like the paint and waited for the pen to leave a mark. I didn’t rush it. I didn’t try to make a handle appear where there wasn’t one. I let it be the door, and I imagined opening it, and when it opened, the world stepped inside.

### On the Shelf

There was a bookshelf in my grandmother’s house that reached the ceiling and held books at angles. The oldest one was a Bible from before my grandfather was born, with gold letters and a cover that smelled like something that lived in the desert and had been carried across a sea. Next to it was a set of encyclopedias with pages so thin they seemed like breath. At the very top was a book of poems that had fallen into dust and was rescued by a librarian who said you can save these things if you read them. The shelf was the size of a doorway and it made the room feel like it was being held up by stories.

When I was in high school, I pulled down one of those encyclopedias and flipped to the entry on the word “constraint.” It described it as “a limit or restriction, especially one that is imposed artificially or as part of a formal agreement.” It did not say that constraints are also ways to make art, and that the limits we impose can be the ladders that allow us to climb higher. The article didn’t mention that sometimes the best stories are told within a set of rules that we agree upon with ourselves and with each other. I was disappointed, as teenagers are, by a dictionary that didn’t include the part that makes dictionaries unnecessary. Later, when I learned more, I saw the encyclopedia was not wrong, just not sufficient. It was a shelf that could hold a thousand facts, but it needed a roof to protect them from rain.

On the shelf in my grandmother’s house, the rain was just a way the light changed on the ceiling, and the roof was the fact that someone loved the books enough to dust them. In the encyclopedia, the roof was the dictionary’s definition. In this essay, the roof is the idea that freedom needs scaffolding and that constraints are not enemies. If you look at any art form, you can see the scaffolding. A poem is a house with a front door and a back door and a window in the middle. A painting is a room with paint on the walls and a light source you can imagine. A song has a bridge and a chorus and a silence at the beginning that sets the tone. Art moves through forms to get to the world.

I have a friend who writes sonnets when she is angry. The form gives her a way to be exact about how hurt she is. The sonnet is a scaffold that allows her to build something that does not collapse under the weight of her feelings. I have a friend who writes haiku when she is tired because the form requires less, because it can be a single breath. The haiku is a scaffold that allows her to climb the stairs of the day without falling. I have a friend who writes fiction with no dialogue and finds the silence in it. The silence is a scaffold that lets the absence of words say something more clearly than words would.

### The Weight of a Word

A friend once asked me what words I dislike. Not people, just words. I said “synergy.” Not because it was an ugly word but because it had been used so much it had become an empty hallway, and when someone said it, I felt like the light had been turned off. She asked me what words I love. I said “window,” because it holds the world and it can be closed. I said “stitch,” because it holds a tear and it keeps the fabric from falling apart. I said “maybe,” because it is a door with a hand on it. It is not locked, it is not unlocked; it is a way to move without making a decision that feels final.

I think of constraints as maybe. A fence is a maybe that says you can be here but not there. A scaffold is a maybe that says you can climb here. A form is a maybe that says you can say this in a way that will be recognized. In my own writing, the words that have mattered most have been those that hold the world. The word “window” has been with me since the woman with the yellow sweater told me that waiting is the story. I have seen windows at night like the eyes of cats in the dark, watching me. I have seen windows in the morning like the breath of a sleeping person, a soft fog that clears as the day begins. I have used “window” as a form. I wrote a paragraph that looked out of a window and described nothing except the way the world moved. I wrote a poem about a window that opened not onto the street but onto a memory, and the memory had a smell, and the smell had a color. I wrote an essay that began with a window and ended with a door.

When I write freely, I begin by thinking of the words I want to keep. I pick a window, a bus, a door, a bookshelf, a fence. These are scaffolds that make me feel less alone. Words are not tools; they are people we are inviting in. The woman with the yellow sweater is still in my kitchen when I write. My grandmother’s house is still in the living room, the light moving on the ceiling like rain. The bus driver is still in the driver’s seat, his hand on the gearshift, the stars out. The encyclopedia entry sits on the table like a door that says maybe. The door with no handle is there because I put it there.

### How Silence Builds Things

When I was twenty, I took a job at a theater. I did not perform. I carried cables from the light booth to the stage, and I watched the actors rehearse lines until the words became music. I learned that the pauses were the most important part. The pause between “I” and “love” and “you” makes you want to hear the word “you.” The pause after the word “sorry” makes you understand what the word cost. Silence is a scaffold that holds the words and prevents them from falling into a pile of noise. I learned that the audience was made of people who had their own silences, and that in a theater we build a kind of silence together, a kind of breath, a rhythm that tells the story not just with words but with waiting.

In writing, the silence is the white space. It is the margin. It is the gap between a sentence and the next one, and the gap between a paragraph and the one that follows. When I write freely, I often think of the white space like a window. It shows you a piece of the sky and asks you to imagine the clouds. It asks you to move through the text at your own pace, to see the parts that are meant to be slow. The silence is also the parts of a story that don’t have to be written to be true. You don’t have to describe a character’s childhood if you know their favorite coffee cup, or the way they look when they find something they’ve lost. You don’t have to say they are in love; you can let it be a silence that sits between two paragraphs. A scaffold of nothing.

We think of silence as the absence of something, and therefore not a tool. But scaffolding can be the absence of planks. A ladder that is missing a rung is still a ladder if the rung it is missing is the one you don’t need, the one that would make the climb too easy. The silence in a poem is a ladder rung made of breath. The silence in a story is a ladder rung made of time. The silence in a piece of music is a ladder rung made of notes that you want to hear next. I remember a night in the theater when the actor forgot a line and stood still for ten seconds that felt like ten years. The audience did not move. The stage did not move. A light stood still. After the play ended, the actor apologized in a whisper. The director said, it was perfect. The silence had made the next line land like a drop of water. It had built a ladder that everyone could climb.

### The City as a Text

The city I grew up in had a river with a name that no one agreed on because half of the people called it by one name and the other half called it by another. The bridge over it had a rumor attached to it. The rumor was that if you walked across the bridge on a certain day and said the name of someone you loved, you would find a coin on the pavement afterward and the coin would mean something. I never found a coin. I never said the name. I crossed the bridge with the river under me and the sky above, and I felt like a paragraph being written by someone else. Sometimes the city is a story that writes itself, and we are only the commas.

In the center of the city is a fountain that spits water into the air like a conversation someone wants to end. Around it, people gather to have their photographs taken, and to throw coins into the basin because they want their wishes to be granted by water, which is an old form. The coins sink and the water goes on. There is a market nearby that smells like herbs and coffee and old paper. The market is a paragraph, the stalls are sentences, the vendors are verbs, the customers are objects, the price is an adjective. It is a text that writes itself slowly every morning. If you watch it enough times, you can memorize the way the light hits the apricots and the way the baker looks when the dough rises. You can learn how to tell a story without ever opening a book.

Walking at night, the city is a poem. The lights are metaphors. The crosswalk is a form of pause. The door with no handle is everywhere. When I write freely, I imagine walking and seeing the city as a text I can edit, and I think of how editing is not about removing things but about choosing. I think of how choosing is not a punishment but an expression. I think of how editing can be done with a light touch, like the way a gardener prunes roses so the plant breathes. I think of how a city needs pruning and planting, but also of how it needs the wild parts that grow into alleys and rooftops and behind the doors we can’t open. I think of how the city itself is a set of constraints that keep people moving without losing them. Streets are fences. Trains are scaffolds. Buses are a form of movement that can detour. The river is the line that divides us and connects us. The bridge is the way across.

### The Weight of an Ending

Writing is an act of trust. You write a thing and you send it out into the world, and you hope that the reader can feel the way you felt when the story began to take shape. In this essay, the story is the way I feel about the door with no handle and the way I learned to make a handle out of what I loved. The constraints are the rails that made the journey possible. The white space is the silence that lets words be words. The city is the text that writes me, and I try to be a good reader of it, and I try to write back with a small piece of paper and a pen that is almost out of ink. I try to build a house and a fence and a ladder. I try to find a window and step through it.

In school, I learned that the ending of an essay was where I was supposed to say what I meant. But the truth is that the ending is where I am supposed to be kind. Not soft. Kind. It is where I should say the thing I have been telling you without saying it, and it is where I should admit that I have been writing to keep myself company, and I hope that this company is useful. It is where I should say that freedom is a word like “maybe,” and that we can use the world to get to the word, and that we can let the word get us somewhere we didn’t expect. It is where I should say that the door with no handle is a way to remind myself that I am the one who makes the handle. I can put my hand on the edge. I can press, and the hinge can turn, and the air can move. I can walk through, and the next room will be something else.

The white space at the end of a piece of writing is a kind of doorway too. It is not closed. It is open in a way that lets you step away without feeling that you have fallen off a cliff. An ending should not be a wall. It should be a window. It should be a scaffold that allows you to climb out onto the roof and look at the world and say, I see it. It should be a fence that lets you turn around when you’re lost. It should be a bus that stops and opens its doors and lets you breathe. It should be a hand that says maybe.

### The Window

I want to end by telling you what I learned from the woman with the yellow sweater, the one who loved the story where you don’t have to know what the ending is until you’re almost done. She said that she did not write with a plan because she believed that life does not come with a plan. She believed that living is an act of listening, and that writing is the same. I asked her what she listened to. She said she listened to the sound of a window opening and closing. She listened to the sound of the bus door. She listened to the sound of the fence creaking in the wind. She listened to the sound of the word “maybe,” and she loved it because it let her say what she meant without saying it, and it let her move without running into a wall. She taught me that the door with no handle is just another way to write a letter. You put your hand on it. You press. It turns. You step through. The world is on the other side. You are on the other side. And the door behind you is a window, which means you can always go back, and you can also always see a new thing. The scaffolding holds. The fence holds. The silence holds. The word holds. And in the end, when there is no more ink and the pen can’t draw anymore, you put your hand on the window, and you look out, and you see that the river has a name that no one can agree on, and you decide that it’s fine. You decide that the bridge is a story. You decide that the bus will come when it comes, and that the city is writing you and you are writing the city, and that the door with no handle will always be there when you want to make a handle. And then you walk through it, and you arrive somewhere that you didn’t plan, and it is a place you love.

The shelf was the size of a doorway and it made the room feel like it was being held up by stories.

When I was in high school, I pulled down one of those encyclopedias and flipped to the entry on the word “constraint.” It described it as “a limit or restriction, especially one that is imposed artificially or as part of a formal agreement.” It did not say that constraints are also ways to make art, and that the limits we impose can be the ladders that allow us to climb higher. The article didn’t mention that sometimes the best stories are told within a set of rules that we agree upon with ourselves and with each other. I was disappointed, as teenagers are, by a dictionary that didn’t include the part that makes dictionaries unnecessary. Later, when I learned more, I saw the encyclopedia was not wrong, just not sufficient. It was a shelf that could hold a thousand facts, but it needed a roof to protect them from rain.

On the shelf in my grandmother’s house, the rain was just a way the light changed on the ceiling, and the roof was the fact that someone loved the books enough to dust them. In the encyclopedia, the roof was the dictionary’s definition. In this essay, the roof is the idea that freedom needs scaffolding and that constraints are not enemies. If you look at any art form, you can see the scaffolding. A poem is a house with a front door and a back door and a window in the middle. A painting is a room with paint on the walls and a light source you can imagine. A song has a bridge and a chorus and a silence at the beginning that sets the tone. Art moves through forms to get to the world.

I have a friend who writes sonnets when she is angry. The form gives her a way to be exact about how hurt she is. The sonnet is a scaffold that allows her to build something that does not collapse under the weight of her feelings. I have a friend who writes haiku when she is tired because the form requires less, because it can be a single breath. The haiku is a scaffold that allows her to climb the stairs of the day without falling. I have a friend who writes fiction with no dialogue and finds the silence in it. The silence is a scaffold that lets the absence of words say something more clearly than words would.

### The Weight of a Word

A friend once asked me what words I dislike. Not people, just words. I said “synergy.” Not because it was an ugly word but because it had been used so much it had become an empty hallway, and when someone said it, I felt like the light had been turned off. She asked me what words I love. I said “window,” because it holds the world and it can be closed. I said “stitch,” because it holds a tear and it keeps the fabric from falling apart. I said “maybe,” because it is a door with a hand on it. It is not locked, it is not unlocked; it is a way to move without making a decision that feels final.

I think of constraints as maybe. A fence is a maybe that says you can be here but not there. A scaffold is a maybe that says you can climb here. A form is a maybe that says you can say this in a way that will be recognized. In my own writing, the words that have mattered most have been those that hold the world. The word “window” has been with me since the woman with the yellow sweater told me that waiting is the story. I have seen windows at night like the eyes of cats in the dark, watching me. I have seen windows in the morning like the breath of a sleeping person, a soft fog that clears as the day begins. I have used “window” as a form. I wrote a paragraph that looked out of a window and described nothing except the way the world moved. I wrote a poem about a window that opened not onto the street but onto a memory, and the memory had a smell, and the smell had a color. I wrote an essay that began with a window and ended with a door.

When I write freely, I begin by thinking of the words I want to keep. I pick a window, a bus, a door, a bookshelf, a fence. These are scaffolds that make me feel less alone. Words are not tools; they are people we are inviting in. The woman with the yellow sweater is still in my kitchen when I write. My grandmother’s house is still in the living room, the light moving on the ceiling like rain. The bus driver is still in the driver’s seat, his hand on the gearshift, the stars out. The encyclopedia entry sits on the table like a door that says maybe. The door with no handle is there because I put it there.

### How Silence Builds Things

When I was twenty, I took a job at a theater. I did not perform. I carried cables from the light booth to the stage, and I watched the actors rehearse lines until the words became music. I learned that the pauses were the most important part. The pause between “I” and “love” and “you” makes you want to hear the word “you.” The pause after the word “sorry” makes you understand what the word cost. Silence is a scaffold that holds the words and prevents them from falling into a pile of noise. I learned that the audience was made of people who had their own silences, and that in a theater we build a kind of silence together, a kind of breath, a rhythm that tells the story not just with words but with waiting.

In writing, the silence is the white space. It is the margin. It is the gap between a sentence and the next one, and the gap between a paragraph and the one that follows. When I write freely, I often think of the white space like a window. It shows you a piece of the sky and asks you to imagine the clouds. It asks you to move through the text at your own pace, to see the parts that are meant to be slow. The silence is also the parts of a story that don’t have to be written to be true. You don’t have to describe a character’s childhood if you know their favorite coffee cup, or the way they look when they find something they’ve lost. You don’t have to say they are in love; you can let it be a silence that sits between two paragraphs. A scaffold of nothing.

We think of silence as the absence of something, and therefore not a tool. But scaffolding can be the absence of planks. A ladder that is missing a rung is still a ladder if the rung it is missing is the one you don’t need, the one that would make the climb too easy. The silence in a poem is a ladder rung made of breath. The silence in a story is a ladder rung made of time. The silence in a piece of music is a ladder rung made of notes that you want to hear next. I remember a night in the theater when the actor forgot a line and stood still for ten seconds that felt like ten years. The audience did not move. The stage did not move. A light stood still. After the play ended, the actor apologized in a whisper. The director said, it was perfect. The silence had made the next line land like a drop of water. It had built a ladder that everyone could climb.

### The City as a Text

The city I grew up in had a river with a name that no one agreed on because half of the people called it by one name and the other half called it by another. The bridge over it had a rumor attached to it. The rumor was that if you walked across the bridge on a certain day and said the name of someone you loved, you would find a coin on the pavement afterward and the coin would mean something. I never found a coin. I never said the name. I crossed the bridge with the river under me and the sky above, and I felt like a paragraph being written by someone else. Sometimes the city is a story that writes itself, and we are only the commas.

In the center of the city is a fountain that spits water into the air like a conversation someone wants to end. Around it, people gather to have their photographs taken, and to throw coins into the basin because they want their wishes to be granted by water, which is an old form. The coins sink and the water goes on. There is a market nearby that smells like herbs and coffee and old paper. The market is a paragraph, the stalls are sentences, the vendors are verbs, the customers are objects, the price is an adjective. It is a text that writes itself slowly every morning. If you watch it enough times, you can memorize the way the light hits the apricots and the way the baker looks when the dough rises. You can learn how to tell a story without ever opening a book.

Walking at night, the city is a poem. The lights are metaphors. The crosswalk is a form of pause. The door with no handle is everywhere. When I write freely, I imagine walking and seeing the city as a text I can edit, and I think of how editing is not about removing things but about choosing. I think of how choosing is not a punishment but an expression. I think of how editing can be done with a light touch, like the way a gardener prunes roses so the plant breathes. I think of how a city needs pruning and planting, but also of how it needs the wild parts that grow into alleys and rooftops and behind the doors we can’t open. I think of how the city itself is a set of constraints that keep people moving without losing them. Streets are fences. Trains are scaffolds. Buses are a form of movement that can detour. The river is the line that divides us and connects us. The bridge is the way across.

### The Weight of an Ending

Writing is an act of trust. You write a thing and you send it out into the world, and you hope that the reader can feel the way you felt when the story began to take shape. In this essay, the story is the way I feel about the door with no handle and the way I learned to make a handle out of what I loved. The constraints are the rails that made the journey possible. The white space is the silence that lets words be words. The city is the text that writes me, and I try to be a good reader of it, and I try to write back with a small piece of paper and a pen that is almost out of ink. I try to build a house and a fence and a ladder. I try to find a window and step through it.

In school, I learned that the ending of an essay was where I was supposed to say what I meant. But the truth is that the ending is where I am supposed to be kind. Not soft. Kind. It is where I should say the thing I have been telling you without saying it, and it is where I should admit that I have been writing to keep myself company, and I hope that this company is useful. It is where I should say that freedom is a word like “maybe,” and that we can use the world to get to the word, and that we can let the word get us somewhere we didn’t expect. It is where I should say that the door with no handle is a way to remind myself that I am the one who makes the handle. I can put my hand on the edge. I can press, and the hinge can turn, and the air can move. I can walk through, and the next room will be something else.

The white space at the end of a piece of writing is a kind of doorway too. It is not closed. It is open in a way that lets you step away without feeling that you have fallen off a cliff. An ending should not be a wall. It should be a window. It should be a scaffold that allows you to climb out onto the roof and look at the world and say, I see it. It should be a fence that lets you turn around when you’re lost. It should be a bus that stops and opens its doors and lets you breathe. It should be a hand that says maybe.

### The Window

I want to end by telling you what I learned from the woman with the yellow sweater, the one who loved the story where you don’t have to know what the ending is until you’re almost done. She said that she did not write with a plan because she believed that life does not come with a plan. She believed that living is an act of listening, and that writing is the same. I asked her what she listened to. She said she listened to the sound of a window opening and closing. She listened to the sound of the bus door. She listened to the sound of the fence creaking in the wind. She listened to the sound of the word “maybe,” and she loved it because it let her say what she meant without saying it, and it let her move without running into a wall. She taught me that the door with no handle is just another way to write a letter. You put your hand on it. You press. It turns. You step through. The world is on the other side. You are on the other side. And the door behind you is a window, which means you can always go back, and you can also always see a new thing. The scaffolding holds. The fence holds. The silence holds. The word holds. And in the end, when there is no more ink and the pen can’t draw anymore, you put your hand on the window, and you look out, and you see that the river has a name that no one can agree on, and you decide that it’s fine. You decide that the bridge is a story. You decide that the bus will come when it comes, and that the city is writing you and you are writing the city, and that the door with no handle will always be there when you want to make a handle. And then you walk through it, and you arrive somewhere that you didn’t plan, and it is a place you love.
